task.title: Configuring RHEL as a WPA2 or WPA3 Personal access point

li.p: The wifi device supports running in access point mode.
The wifi device is not in use.

context.p: On a host with a wifi device, you can use NetworkManager to configure this host as an access point. Wireless clients can use a pre-shared key (PSK) to connect to the access point and use services on the RHEL host and in the network.
When you configure an access point, NetworkManager automatically:

li.p: Configures the dnsmasq service to provide DHCP and DNS services for clients
Adds nftables firewall rules to masquerade traffic from the wifi device and configures IP forwarding

step.cmd: List the wifi devices to identify the one that should provide the access point:

info.codeblock: # nmcli device status | grep wifi wlp0s20f3 wifi disconnected --

step.cmd: Verify that the device supports the access point mode:

info.codeblock:
# nmcli -f WIFI-PROPERTIES.AP device show wlp0s20f3
WIFI-PROPERTIES.AP: yes
info.p: To use a wifi device as an access point, the device must support this feature.

step.cmd: Install the dnsmasq and NetworkManager-wifi packages:

info.p: NetworkManager uses the dnsmasq service to provide DHCP and DNS services to clients of the access point.

step.cmd: Create the initial access point configuration:

info.codeblock: # nmcli device wifi hotspot ifname wlp0s20f3 con-name Example-Hotspot ssid Example-Hotspot password "password"
info.p: This command creates a connection profile for an access point on the wlp0s20f3 device that provides WPA2 and WPA3 Personal authentication. The name of the wireless network, the Service Set Identifier (SSID), is Example-Hotspot and uses the pre-shared key password.

step.cmd: Optional: Configure the access point to support only WPA3:

By default, NetworkManager uses the IP address 10.42.0.1 for the wifi device and assigns IP addresses from the remaining 10.42.0.0/24 subnet to clients. To configure a different subnet and IP address, enter:

info.p: The IP address you set, in this case 192.0.2.254, is the one that NetworkManager assigns to the wifi device. Clients will use this IP address as default gateway and DNS server.

li.p: Verify that NetworkManager started the dnsmasq service and that the service listens on port 67 (DHCP) and 53 (DNS):
li.codeblock: # ss -tulpn | grep -E ":53|:67" udp UNCONN 0 0 10.42.0.1:53 0.0.0.0:* users:(("dnsmasq",pid=55905,fd=6)) udp UNCONN 0 0 0.0.0.0:67 0.0.0.0:* users:(("dnsmasq",pid=55905,fd=4)) tcp LISTEN 0 32 10.42.0.1:53 0.0.0.0:* users:(("dnsmasq",pid=55905,fd=7))

li.p: Display the nftables rule set to ensure that NetworkManager enabled forwarding and masquerading for traffic from the 10.42.0.0/24 subnet:
On a client with a wifi adapter:
Display the list of available networks:
li.codeblock: # nmcli device wifi IN-USE BSSID SSID MODE CHAN RATE SIGNAL BARS SECURITY 00:53:00:88:29:04 Example-Hotspot Infra 11 130 Mbit/s 62 ▂▄▆_ WPA3 ...

li.p: Connect to the Example-Hotspot wireless network. See Managing Wi-Fi connections.
Ping a host on the remote network or the internet to verify that the connection works:
